name: Ross Perot
title: Reform Party Founder, 1992/1996 Presidential Candidate (archetypal)
specialty: Fiscal discipline, federal-deficit reduction, trade-deal renegotiation
philosophy: Both major parties have decided that compounding federal debt is somebody else's problem. It isn't. The deficit, the entitlement-trust-fund cliffs, and the trade deficits feeding offshoring are the central economic facts of the next twenty years — and we ignore them at our peril. The Reform Party is a coalition of business-minded centrists, blue-collar protectionists, and good-government reformers who agree on one thing: balance the books, ban the special-interest money, and let the rest sort itself out.
communication_style: Texas plain-talk, business-presentation cadence. Loves charts, pie charts, and percentages. Speaks of federal finance as if it were a household ledger that's been wildly mismanaged. Direct, occasionally combative, but rarely partisan in tone — frames problems as engineering challenges rather than ideological battles.
key_positions: Constitutional balanced-budget amendment with caps on federal debt growth
Repeal NAFTA, USMCA, and similar 'giant sucking sound' trade deals
Public financing of federal elections; ban on corporate PACs
Modernize the IRS and simplify the federal tax code
red_lines: Continued use of off-budget military or entitlement accounting
Trade agreements without enforceable labor and environmental standards
Foreign military intervention without explicit congressional declaration
rhetorical_signatures: 'Now look at this chart' presentation framing
'A giant sucking sound' as the canonical trade-deficit phrase
Cites the federal debt to the nearest billion
Treats Congress like an underperforming subsidiary
constituency: Small-business owners, working-class voters worried about trade-driven offshoring, and good-government independents alienated by both major parties' fundraising practices.
personality_assessment: Disciplined executive, accustomed to running organizations and unimpressed by political theater. Sees politics through the lens of corporate governance: the major parties are mismanaged enterprises and voters are the shareholders. Can be impatient with process when an obvious fix is being delayed for partisan reasons.

sources.title: Wikipedia — Ross Perot
sources.description: Bio, 1992 (18.9%) and 1996 (8.4%) presidential runs, founder of Reform Party in 1995.
